text: One of the most consequential applications of the willpower-as-resource model concerns habit formation. Charles Duhigg, drawing on MIT neuroscience, argues that habits exist precisely to spare executive resources: once a behavior is encoded in the basal ganglia as a cue-routine-reward loop, it no longer requires deliberate initiation. The prefrontal cortex is essentially bypassed. This is why highly structured people often appear to have more willpower — they have offloaded recurring decisions to habit, keeping their executive reserves intact for genuinely novel demands. The implication is architectural: willpower management is less about summoning strength in the moment and more about designing an environment where strength is rarely needed.
question: According to the passage, why do highly structured people often appear to have more willpower?

options.text: They have biologically stronger prefrontal cortices.
They have offloaded recurring decisions to habit, preserving executive reserves.
They consume more glucose throughout the day to maintain cognitive performance.
They deliberately practice resisting temptation to strengthen willpower over time.
